item.title: Unlock the full potential of your workspace with this impressive 17-in-1 Thunderbolt 5 docking station that's $110 off
item.itunes:title: Unlock the full potential of your workspace with this impressive 17-in-1 Thunderbolt 5 docking station that's $110 off
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
If you're looking to go all out, this is the docking station you need. This new addition to Ugreen's growing lineup delivers impressive connectivity, along with lightning-fast speeds with support for Thunderbolt 5. Naturally, this 17-in-1 docking station isn't going to come cheap, but you can now grab it for less thanks to a steep discount that knocks $110 off.

What's great about this Ugreen 17-in-1 Thunderbolt 5 docking station?

Credit: UGREEN

When it comes to docking stations, it's really going to be all about port selection. If it doesn't have the ports you need for your workflow, then there's a pretty good chance that it won't be worth the investment. With that said, this Ugreen Thunderbolt 5 docking station really delivers when it comes to speed and port selection.

You get 17 ports in total, with the front panel housing three USB-C ports, with two of those ports providing up to 60W of charging power. There's also a 3.5mm audio jack, SD, and microSD card slots. The rear panel is where things get more interesting, with three Thunderbolt 5 ports, DisplayPort 2.1 with 8K output, Ethernet, three USB-A, a 3.5mm audio jack, and a microphone jack.

In addition to the above, the docking station also has room for SSD expansion and provides adequate cooling to keep things running smoothly. For the most part, these ports are going to be enough for most people. However, as stated before, these ports may not suit your workflow, so you'll want to check and see before making your purchase.

Subscribe for more gear deals, reviews, and picks

Get deeper gear coverage—subscribe to our newsletter for curated breakdowns, hands-on analysis, and deal roundups on docking stations, peripherals, and other tech, so you’ll have clear recommendations and buying context when evaluating options.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

Overall, we like what Ugreen is offering here. The brand has been fairly aggressive in the computing space over the past year, and it's delivering some great products at excellent prices. Grab this 17-in-1 docking station for a reduced price while you can.

UGREEN Revodok Maxidok 17-in-1 Thunderbolt 5 Docking Station
$390 $500 Save $110
Ports
6x Thunderbolt 5; 1x DisplayPort; 3x USB-A ; 5x USB-C (10Gbps); 1x Ethernet; 1x SD Card Slot
Maximum display resolution
8K
Extra features
Built-in M.2 Storage Expansion

If you're looking for a lightning-quick docking station that can do it all, this one from Ugreen might be a great option. While it's normally priced at $500, it can now be had for much less, with a steep discount that knocks $110 off.

$390 at Ugreen

item.title: 4 Claude Code slash commands I use daily that make me more productive
item.itunes:title: 4 Claude Code slash commands I use daily that make me more productive
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
I use Claude Code in two very different ways: managing my home lab server and building web apps. Most people treat Claude as a smart terminal and focus only on prompts. In a few instances, treating it like a chatbot means missing built-in controls like slash commands.

The slash commands are one of the most under-used features that Claude Code ships with and can substantially improve workflow efficiency. Slash commands aren’t Easter eggs; they are useful built-in commands that I use daily to be more efficient and productive. If you’re not moving beyond the basic prompts, you are missing out on a layer that fundamentally changes how you interact with it.

Here are a few slash commands that I rely on every day:

I set up Claude Code the way its creator does, and the difference is night and day

Who better to learn from than the person who made it?

18
By  Mahnoor Faisal

— /model

Switching brains mid-task is more powerful than it sounds

Most Claude users stick to one model for everything, whether it’s brainstorming a complex idea, planning implementation, or handling casual prompts. That usually means the model is either overkill or underpowered for the task. In the Claude app, switching the model forces you into a new session, breaking the flow; the Claude Code CLI handles it differently. It ships with a built-in /model command to switch instantly without breaking the flow.

This is one of my most-used commands when I am deep in development. When starting a new project, I begin with the Haiku model for quick exploration (cheap and fast) and then switch to the Opus model for deeper reasoning (design, algo, and architecture). Once I have a solid plan, I switch to the Sonnet model for execution and iteration (balanced and efficient). Sonnet for execution, since Opus has already done the hard thinking. All of this happens with a single command: /model. You can either browse the available models by executing /model or switch directly by name – /model Opus.

This helps me keep my workflow efficient. Token usage matters too, since you are still limited by tokens under a subscription. Larger models like Opus are best suited for brainstorming complex ideas, but they also consume significantly more tokens. Lighter models like Haiku are more efficient for everyday tasks, whereas Sonnet is a good balance between cost and capability. The real benefit of the /model command isn’t switching models; it’s using the right model at the right time.

— /mcp

This is where Claude stops being just a chatbot

I am a homelab enthusiast and love the hobby; the fun stops when Docker container numbers grow. In my local homelab Debian server, I have 17 containers, each for different services I self-host.

For a simple issue or routine checkup, I had to open Portainer and check each container one by one to diagnose any issues. This is where /mcp comes in. I installed a Docker MCP server inside my homelab, and the /mcp command helps me manage that connection. I can now check which servers are connected, their status, and what tools they expose without leaving the Claude Code interface. The /mcp command not only helps me monitor MCP servers connected to my server locally but also all remote connections tied to my Claude account.

Now, I don’t log into various dashboards like Portainer, Uptime Kuma, Beszel, and Speedtest Tracker just to check status anymore; I ask Claude now. For example, I can ask things like, “What’s running healthy on my server?" or “Do any containers need an update?" The docker-mcp server handles all the queries, and /mcp helps keep the connection managed. Eventually, it stops being a chatbot and starts acting like a neural interface between the server and everything I have connected to it.

— /memory and /context

Stop repeating yourself every single session

Working with LLMs feels like I am repeating myself on every new session. AI assistants like Claude don’t store anything from previous sessions; the project feature in the Claude app keeps a small fragment of context. It also gets pushed out when the conversations grow. Whether I am managing my homelab server or building a web app, I always have to restate the boundaries for the server or workflow instructions for development.

This is where the /memory command helps me by opening and managing my CLAUDE.md file. The file contains my persistent instructions, which Claude reads at the start of every new session. For example, I always keep sensitive containers such as Vaultwarden and the Omada controller either out of context entirely or read-only. The same applies to web development as well: my tech stack preferences, directory conventions, and file nomenclature. This isn’t a hard technical lock but instruction-based enforcement.

The /context command comes in handy when I am using the /memory command. Even with memory, the context can still grow, and sometimes it becomes difficult to manage, especially when I have long sessions. For example, every new session starts with similar sets of instructions, but I also instruct Claude to update the file on every new milestone, and by the end, the context becomes overloaded. What the /context command does is visualize my entire context window and categorize the token usage. It is smart enough to suggest pruning when memory files consume too many tokens. When /context flags the bloat, /compact summarizes the conversation to keep it as context and clears the rest.

— /agents

When one Claude isn’t enough

When working with Claude, there is always a moment when I instruct a multi-step prompt, and I’m stuck waiting for one task to finish before starting the next. Most users use Claude Code in the same pattern, that is, linearly: one prompt, one response, and one task at a time. Managing workflows that require simultaneous tasks gets messy fast. For example, while running a code review, I need to generate an implementation plan for another module. Doing these sequentially takes more time than it should.

Subscribe for deeper Claude Code command guides

Get hands-on Claude Code guidance: subscribe to the newsletter for in-depth slash-command tutorials, practical setups, and configuration examples that show how to use /model, /mcp, /memory, /context and /agents to streamline workflows.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

This is where the /agents command changes the equation completely. The command can spin up a new instance that runs parallel to the previous task without changing the session. These are separate assistants for specific tasks. All these happen in the same running session, which means the agents have all the context of the project. There are two subagents that I use almost every session: code-reviewer and implementation-plan. These are custom subagents that I created via /agents for my development sessions. Both are available across all my projects.

For example, I can use both of them to run simultaneously, invoked with a prompt like: “Use the code-reviewer agent to review my current auth module, and use the implementation-plan agent to draft a plan for the payments module." Both subagents will run in parallel with the same context as the whole project. Though it is extremely useful when used properly, you also need to note that each subagent will consume tokens as per its usage. Multi-agent sessions can exhaust your Pro plan within 15 minutes.

Stop using CLAUDE.md; here's what actually works for AI-assisted development

Do you really need custom context files for every repository?

5
By  Joe Rice-Jones

Stop prompting and start controlling

Most Claude users focus on prompts, which is also the most limited way to use it. When you stop assuming it is a smarter terminal and start treating it as an environment you control, you change the way you interact with it. Slash commands are one of the examples. None of the commands I mentioned are glamorous; you won't find them in any onboarding guide either. But by using these small built-in commands together, you can reduce a lot of friction from your daily workflow. After involving these in my daily workflow, I stopped thinking about prompts and started thinking about control over my workflow.

Claude
OS
Windows, macOS
Individual pricing
Free plan available; $17/month Pro plan

Claude is an AI assistant and a series of large language models developed and maintained by Anthropic.

See at Official Site See at Claude

item.title: Need storage on the cheap? Grab this 512GB USB drive for just $38
item.itunes:title: Need storage on the cheap? Grab this 512GB USB drive for just $38
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
The price of storage has increased quite a bit over the past year, but that doesn't mean there still are good deals out there to be had. If you're looking for simple storage that's cheap and easy to use, then this Lexar JumpDrive is going to be a great solution.

It's a USB-A drive with 512GB of storage. This won't provide the fastest transfer speeds, but at least it's a good amount of storage for just $38. The steep discount knocks 62% off right now, making it a great time to pick one up.

Keep things simple

SSD drives are going to be the way to go if you're looking for fast and reliable storage. However, with prices going through the roof, we think it's a good time to look at alternative solutions. That's where this Lexar USB drive comes in, with 512GB of storage and transfer speeds that can reach up to 150MB/s.

The drive is durable, and there's even a built-in cover that will help protect the USB-A connection. You can even keep the data safe with 256-bit AES encryption. For the most part, it's a pretty straightforward drive that makes storage simple. If you need more storage space and speed, you'll need to go with something else, which can be more expensive.

Join our newsletter for smart storage deals and tips

Subscribe to the newsletter to get curated storage deals and practical buying guidance, from USB drives to SSDs, plus coverage of related tech deals, product comparisons and discount highlights to help you decide.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

But, if this is something that will work for your needs, we recommend grabbing this USB drive while you can at this discounted price. It's the lowest price we've seen all year, making it the perfect time to pick one up.

Lexar JumpDrive
$38 $100 Save $62

If you're looking for cheap data storage, this Lexar USB drive is going to be it. You get 512GB of storage for just $38 right now.

$38 at Amazon

item.title: Windows 11 buries its best features for power-users deep behind the worst menus
item.itunes:title: Windows 11 buries its best features for power-users deep behind the worst menus
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Windows 11 looks clean, modern, and far more approachable than any version of Windows before it. That being said, it still has its problems, and the worst part is that a lot of them have been inherited over generations. The polish is surface-level, while underneath, it's the same, sprawling, layered operating system it's always been, except now the pathways to its most powerful features are harder to navigate than ever.

In fact, I wouldn't say that Windows 11 lacks capability at all, but the problem lies in the fact that Microsoft continues to bury that capability under mismatched menus, legacy tools, and half-finished transitions that have dragged on for years. The power is all there, but the problem is that you're expected to already know where to look.

7 design flaws in Windows 11 Microsoft still hasn't fixed

What are we waiting for?

15
By  João Carrasqueira

The Control Panel vs. Settings is still an identity crisis

Where does one really need to go?

Windows 11 still feels like two, three, or four operating systems stitched together without a clear plan for which one should take priority. You've got the modern Settings app, which looks great and covers the basics well enough. Then, you have that old Control Panel, which is still very much alive, still very necessary, and still hiding critical functionality that never made the jump.

This creates a weird scavenger hunt where you'd be constantly bouncing between generations of UI just to complete a single task. Between power plans, advanced network settings, and optional features, you'll have to either memorize their locations or waste time digging through layers that don't always lead where you expect. The "Optional Features" section is a perfect example: you need to know something exists before you can even enable it.

I've always believed it's better to disable what you don't need than to hunt for features you didn't even know were there in the first place. Windows 11 flips that philosophy on its head, and it punishes anyone who isn't already familiar with its quirks.

Windows 11's design lead says Microsoft plans to modernize legacy features like Control Panel and Device Manager

The tech giant is "building out tooling to scale modernizing other dialogs across Windows 11."

7
By  Patrick O'Rourke

The taskbar still gets in your way more than it helps

Even the context menu fights 30 years of muscle memory

If there's one part of Windows 11 that perfectly captures this problem, it's the taskbar. It's been controversial since day one, and while Microsoft has slowly walked back some of its worst decisions, it still feels like a downgrade in terms of usability. The biggest offender is the right-click context menu. What used to be a fast, muscle-memory-driven interaction is now split into two layers: a simplified menu up front, and the actual useful options tucked behind "Show more options." That is decades of workflow efficiency buried behind an extra click for no good reason.

This isn't just change for the sake of change, either. It's just friction at this point, and it adds up quickly when you're doing real work. Thankfully, you can restore the classic context menu with a registry tweak, or use a tool like WinAero Tweaker that lets you restore it with a single click. The problem, however, is the fact that you shouldn't have to fix your OS just to get back basic functionality.

Storage and sound settings are split across different eras

It's always decades-old menus in the end

Windows 11's storage settings look downright fantastic, don't they? They have clean graphs, simple breakdowns, and easy toggles, all for the sake of the OS's modern, pretty UI. However, the moment you need any actual control, you're going to head right back into legacy territory. Disk Management is still the real tool for anything meaningful, since that's where you can partition drives, assign letters, and manage volumes. Where do you find it, though? It's nowhere in the polished Storage page, but hidden behind a right-click on the Start menu like some kind of secret shortcut.

Oh, and don't even get me started on Sound settings. Changing output devices is straightforward, and I sure do love the Ctrl + Win + V shortcut, but the moment I need to tweak sample rates, enable exclusive mode, or properly configure a DAC, I have to dig four layers deep to Settings → System → Sound → More sound settings. And at the end of that journey is a Windows 95-style dialog box that looks completely out of place in 2026.

5 reasons why a wireless receiver replaced my USB dongle DAC

Good audio doesn't equal great sacrifice

1
By  Chandraveer Mathur

Sysinternals is insanely powerful, and also practically invisible

Then there's Sysinternals, which might just be the most powerful suite of tools that Microsoft offers, and the vast majority of users have never even heard of it. We're talking about over 70 utilities that let you monitor processes, analyze system behavior, manage startup entries, and troubleshoot issues at a level far beyond that Task Manager can handle. Tools like Process Explorer and Autoruns are invaluable in real-world scenarios.

If you need to figure out why your system is slowing down, or need to track down a stubborn background process, you'll be able to use Sysinternals for all of that, and then some. The catch, however, is that it's not built into Windows in any visible way at all. You have to know it exists, go out of your way to download it, and then learn how to use it. For something this powerful, that feels like a missed opportunity. Most users don't tweak what they don't know they can tweak, after all.

You don’t need a clean Windows install — you need better defaults

A complete reset is rarely the answer anymore

4
By  Samarveer Singh

PowerToys and God Mode are also hidden tools

Swiss army knives that you have to find first

Windows 11 has a way of making you feel like you can do so much more with PowerToys, which is an incredible collection of utilities. It has FancyZones for window management, PowerRename for bulk file edits, Keyboard Manager for remapping keys, and so much more, which genuinely make daily workflows smoother and faster. Then, there's the "God Mode," which essentially dumps a massive list of system settings into one accessible folder, giving you a level of control that is genuinely impressive.

And yet, again, none of this is front and center at all. PowerToys has to be downloaded separately from the Windows Store. Even God Mode has to be manually enabled. It essentially gives you a single folder that lists hundreds of advanced system settings in one place, from administrative tools to deep configuration options that are otherwise scattered across Control Panel and Settings. This is what helps you cut through Windows 11's messy menus and lets you access everything directly.

To enable God Mode, create a new folder on your desktop and rename it to the following:

Subscribe to the newsletter for Windows 11 hidden power

Stop hunting through menus—subscribe to the newsletter to get clear, practical Windows 11 guides, deep dives into hidden tools like Sysinternals and PowerToys, and concise how-tos that help you find and use features other guides bury.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

GodMode.{ED7BA470-8E54-465E-825C-99712043E01C}

It will immediately change to a Control Panel icon and list all the PC settings inside. These aren't experimental features at all, but they are still hidden because Microsoft simply doesn't surface them. That's the recurring theme here: the best parts of Windows 11 feel like secrets, hidden behind elbow grease, legwork, and menus.

Windows 11 Pro

A USB installation drive and license key for Windows 11 Pro, with additional features like Hyper-V and Windows Sandbox support.

See at Amazon See at Best Buy See at Microsoft
6 features worth enabling God Mode in Windows 11 for

If you want access to over 200 features in Windows 11 without complex navigation, use these features in God Mode.

By  Brian Burgess

Windows 11 has the power, but you just have to dig for it

This is so much more of a design problem rather than a limitation problem.

Windows 11 is not lacking in capability. If anything, it's one of the most flexible and powerful operating systems you can use today. But that power is scattered across menus, buried behind legacy interfaces, and locked away behind features you’re expected to discover on your own.

This is so much more of a design problem rather than a limitation problem. Microsoft's OS does have incredible capabilities, but instead of bringing that power forward, it hides them behind layers of inconsistency and outdated design. Ultimately, Windows 11 isn't an OS that serves you best out of the box, but one you have to spend time in, digging for features to reclaim. It's time we stopped settling for the "polished" surface and start demanding an interface that actually trusts its users with the tools they need.

item.title: Gmail’s useful new AI inbox features will cost you $250 a month
item.itunes:title: Gmail’s useful new AI inbox features will cost you $250 a month
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Summary

  • Gmail's new AI Inbox shows personalized briefings and interactive cards for key emails and tasks.
  • Access is limited to US Google AI Ultra subscribers at $250/month, so I won't be trying it anytime soon.
  • Google may bring it to cheaper tiers (like $20 AI Pro) — I'm still uneasy about giving AI deep access to my data.

Following the feature's reveal back in January 2026 and initial testing with a small group, Google has rolled out Gmail's AI integration.

As far as AI features go, the ability to access a personalized briefing that pulls up key tasks and highlights important emails related to an upcoming bill, a doctor's appointment, or a vacation that's just around the corner, is pretty useful and the kind of thing I'd really appreciate. All of this information appears in easy-to-view interactive cards alongside your regular inbox. I'm constantly drowning in what feels like never-ending emails, so any tool that helps me parse through my messy inbox more easily is something I'm interested in.

Unfortunately, AI Inbox is currently limited to Google AI Ultra subscribers in the US, which costs an astounding $250/month — so there's no way I'm trying it any time soon.

Gmail's potentially useful AI features will likely make their way to a lower subscription tier soon

I'm still hesitant to give AI this much access to my personal information

Of course, Google's AI Ultra subscription tier also includes features beyond Gmail's clever AI integration, including higher Gemini usage limits, more advanced AI models, and 30TB of cloud storage across Drive, Photos, and other Google apps. You also get access to YouTube Premium and Google Home Premium Advanced.

Sure, that's a lot of stuff, but at $250, which comes to $3,000 a year, it should be.

Thankfully, Google is expected to bring this feature to more users in the future, which hopefully includes Google's far more reasonable $20/month AI Pro tier. At the same time, I'm still hesitant to give AI this level of access to sensitive personal information.

That said, the tech giant has claimed in the past that, from a privacy perspective, its new AI Gmail features offer the "privacy protections you expect from Google," though it's still unclear what that actually means.

Google wants to reinvent Gmail's inbox with AI — and some features actually sound useful

If you're as tired of hunting through you Gmail inbox as I am, Smart Overviews might be the feature you've been waiting for.

By  Patrick O'Rourke
item.description:
Summary

  • Gmail's new AI Inbox shows personalized briefings and interactive cards for key emails and tasks.
  • Access is limited to US Google AI Ultra subscribers at $250/month, so I won't be trying it anytime soon.
  • Google may bring it to cheaper tiers (like $20 AI Pro) — I'm still uneasy about giving AI deep access to my data.

Following the feature's reveal back in January 2026 and initial testing with a small group, Google has rolled out Gmail's AI integration.

As far as AI features go, the ability to access a personalized briefing that pulls up key tasks and highlights important emails related to an upcoming bill, a doctor's appointment, or a vacation that's just around the corner, is pretty useful and the kind of thing I'd really appreciate. All of this information appears in easy-to-view interactive cards alongside your regular inbox. I'm constantly drowning in what feels like never-ending emails, so any tool that helps me parse through my messy inbox more easily is something I'm interested in.

Unfortunately, AI Inbox is currently limited to Google AI Ultra subscribers in the US, which costs an astounding $250/month — so there's no way I'm trying it any time soon.

Gmail's potentially useful AI features will likely make their way to a lower subscription tier soon

I'm still hesitant to give AI this much access to my personal information

Of course, Google's AI Ultra subscription tier also includes features beyond Gmail's clever AI integration, including higher Gemini usage limits, more advanced AI models, and 30TB of cloud storage across Drive, Photos, and other Google apps. You also get access to YouTube Premium and Google Home Premium Advanced.

Sure, that's a lot of stuff, but at $250, which comes to $3,000 a year, it should be.

Thankfully, Google is expected to bring this feature to more users in the future, which hopefully includes Google's far more reasonable $20/month AI Pro tier. At the same time, I'm still hesitant to give AI this level of access to sensitive personal information.

That said, the tech giant has claimed in the past that, from a privacy perspective, its new AI Gmail features offer the "privacy protections you expect from Google," though it's still unclear what that actually means.

Google wants to reinvent Gmail's inbox with AI — and some features actually sound useful

If you're as tired of hunting through you Gmail inbox as I am, Smart Overviews might be the feature you've been waiting for.

By  Patrick O'Rourke

item.title: Intel's New Arc Pro GPUs show what the consumer cards should have been
item.itunes:title: Intel's New Arc Pro GPUs show what the consumer cards should have been
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
When Intel unveiled the Arc Pro B70 and Arc Pro B65 GPUs, it also left hardware enthusiasts with a special kind of frustration that's reserved for hardware that exists just outside their reach. Not because they're too expensive (although the Arc Pro B70's $949 MSRP hardly puts it in the impulse-purchase territory), but because they were never meant for the average consumer in the first place.

At Pro Day 2026, Intel's two new Battlemage GPUs built on the Xe2 architecture, featuring 32 Xe cores, 32GB of GDDR6 memory and 608 GB/s bandwidth offered exactly the kind of high-end proposition that the market had been waiting for from Team Blue, just not for the audience that had been awaiting their arrival.

The "Big Battlemage" cards offer large bandwidth and VRAM

For creators and AI developers

Credit: Intel

The new Arc Pro B70 is a workstation card, and a serious contender against Nvidia's RTX Pro 4000. Its 32GB VRAM buffer makes it highly capable of running local LLMs, and at the $949 price-point against the equivalent Blackwell card's $1,800, Team Blue is making that challenge with some conviction.

Intel's own figures claim up to 2.2x larger context windows versus the competition, up to 6.2x faster responses across multi-user workloads, and up to 2x better tokens-per-dollar performance. The card supports a power envelope ranging from 160W-290W, which makes it able to accommodate a range of cooling configurations and form-factors.

The Arc Pro B65, which was announced alongside it, retains the 32GB VRAM budget but scales down to 20 Xe cores. Intel hasn't announced a price for the B65 yet, although early estimates seem to suggest it may fall between $600-$800. Team Blue has time and again surprised the market with their pricing and undercutting strategies, so it wouldn't be entirely unusual to see the B65 arrive closer to the lower end of that range.

3 reasons I really want Intel to release another Battlemage GPU

We all need the Intel Battlemage train to keep going

1
By  Tanveer Singh

Battlemage at high-end was always the GPU gamers needed

The GPU that "could have been" the B770

Earlier this year, the rumors of an Arc B770 discrete GPU had consumers watching Intel's next move intently, especially after the success of the B-series GPUs in the market, which, in part, contributed to Team Blue winning 1% of the intensely-competitive GPU space. However, the exciting prospect of a consumer-facing version of "Big Battlemage" was indeed short-lived when leaked reports confirmed what many had begun to suspect. Intel had shelved the Arc B770 citing financial viability concerns, without any announcements or official statements.

The Arc B580, which is currently the most capable consumer Battlemage card available, runs on 20 Xe cores. The B70, on the other hand, has 32, paired with a memory capacity that no other gaming GPU at this price point comes close to matching. Nvidia's current-gen mid-range champion, the RTX 5070, ships with 12GB of GDDR7, whereas the B70, built for inference workloads, outmatches it entirely with its massive 32GB VRAM buffer.

It's hard not to notice that in the $400-$600 range, a consumer-oriented GPU with this memory configuration (perhaps with fewer Xe cores) would have made the Arc impossible to ignore for enthusiasts who've watched newer cards release with 8GB of VRAM, making memory a frustrating subject of conversation every year. The hardware that could've been the B770 for consumers is now tailored towards AI and ML workloads, missing all gaming optimizations, and given the paradigm, it's not even a surprise.

Has Intel given up on gaming GPUs?

Not just yet, but they're focused on greener pastures

Credit: Source: Intel

The reason why Intel's decision not to launch a gaming version of the "Big Battlemage" doesn't come as a surprise is because their strategy has been clear, so far. CEO Lip-Bu Tan has been candid about where Team Blue currently stands. The company is orienting itself around edge AI and focusing on bringing AI processing directly to consumers, rather than competing for workloads it no longer believes it has a competitive edge in. Whereas professional and workstation markets fit neatly into that vision, gaming currently does not.

The economic situation that the memory market currently finds itself in also leaves little room for gamers. With VRAM costs at historic highs, the cost of a high-VRAM consumer card has become increasingly difficult to justify, even more so for a division with a little over 1% of the discrete GPU market segment and limited runway to absorb a misstep.

More great hardware, but not for gamers

Intel's new Arc Pro lineup shows exactly what the Battlemage GPUs are capable of at the high-end. Impressive VRAM allocation, strong throughput, and highly competitive pricing. Their absence from the gaming market does indeed, however, signal a shift in priorities, and that was to be expected. For enthusiasts, the cards show a glimpse of what the B770 could have been, but it remains to be seen whether Intel intends to finish what Battlemage kicked off.

item.title: Please stop overcomplicating your notes — these lightweight apps do everything Obsidian does
item.itunes:title: Please stop overcomplicating your notes — these lightweight apps do everything Obsidian does
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Obsidian is great, but it also seems like a tool that asks for a lot upfront. Before you’ve written a single note, you’re already making decisions about the vault location, folder structures, and which plugins you need. There’s also a whole ecosystem of guides and YouTube tutorials on how to set up Obsidian the “right way”. It can get overwhelming fast. If you’re the type of person like me who gets stuck in setup mode before doing any actual work, this can be a problem.

Obsidian isn’t actually that complicated to use and you don’t need to be a power user to get value from it - my setup ended up being extremely simple. But I can see how it’s not everyone’s cup of tea, and I also don’t think it has the most amazing UI, which probably drives some people away. There are other options out there that have the same core features and principles as Obsidian, however, and they’re also much easier to navigate. So if Obsidian doesn’t seem like it would be your thing, I recommend checking out these tools…

QOwnNotes

Lightweight, but not barebones

I’m starting off with the least minimalistic option on this list, though it is still quite light and easy to use. QOwnNotes is an open-source cross-platform note-taking app that stores everything in plain text Markdown files on your device. It’s built in C++, making it super lightweight compared to most modern note-takers, and it was originally built around ownCloud integration, though that’s fully optional.

Feature-wise, it covers more ground than most on this list. You get Markdown editing with live preview, tagging, subfolders, full-text search, AES 256 encryption, distraction-free mode, and a browser extension for web clipping. There’s also a scripting engine if you want to customize behavior, and the optional Nextcloud integration for syncing and note versioning across devices. It even has Vim mode if that’s your thing.

Overall, it’s almost as feature-rich as Obsidian but not quite, so there’s less to navigate and you can just focus on creating and managing your notes.

QOwnNotes
See at QOwnNotes

FocusWriter

A truly minimalist note-taking experience

I’ve covered FocusWriter several times already because I just adore it for how distraction-free and easy it is to use. Plus, the aesthetic vibe doesn’t hurt. It’s an open-source word processor developed and maintained by one person, named Graeme Gott. It’s available on Mac, Windows, and Linux and runs locally. The biggest appeal is that the entire interface disappears until you run your cursor to the edge of the screen - it’s just a blank page until then.

The feature set is intentionally minimal. You get autosave, spellcheck, find and replace, daily word and time goals with streak tracking, timers, and real-time stats. By default, it saves your files in ODT format, but it also supports plain text. There’s also a major focus on the look and feel of the app, and you can customize the theme, fonts, colors, and so on.

You’ll probably notice that the functionality is more geared to getting you to focus and write rather than build a knowledge base. So it’s worth keeping that in mind if you’re working with heavy research stacks, in which case, FocusWriter is not the best candidate. But it is an excellent drafting space that makes it easy to focus on whatever it is you’re writing or noting down.

FocusWriter
See at Official Site

Simplenote

It stays true to its name

Despite being almost two decades old, I only discovered Simplenote several months ago, but I’ve been raving about it since. It’s open-source and available on everything, including Windows, Linux, Mac, iOS, Android, and the web. This is a cloud-synced app rather than a local-first one, so you need an account to use it, but it syncs fast and also works offline.

The features are genuinely barebones, which is the whole point. You get a two-pane layout - note list left, editor right - with no toolbar, folders, or formatting buttons. It’s just a blank space to write in. There is Markdown support if you want it but it doesn’t have a live preview that renders the syntax. Tags work for organization if you need them, and version history is pretty solid.

Overall, a very different app from Obsidian. If you’re someone who just needs a simple space for your notes and don’t care about extras like backlinking or dashboard plugins, then Obsidian probably isn’t for you, and something like Simplenote is a much better fit.

Simplenote
See at Simplenote

Joplin

The closest Obsidian alternative

You’ve probably already heard of Joplin, but if not, it’s basically the closest alternative you’ll get to Obsidian. It prioritizes ownership and open standards the same way with its local-first and open standard nature. The look and feel is a little different from Obsidian; its layout is much simpler to navigate and also looks a bit neater.

All your basics are covered. You get tags, notebooks, full-text search, a web clipper, to-do lists with reminders, end-to-end encryption, sync options, and a ton of plugins if you want them (including ones for backlinking and graph views like in Obsidian). It also supports a split Markdown view and live preview mode.

Joplin
See at Joplin

Notes shouldn’t be complicated

Obsidian is worth learning if you want to build a proper knowledge hub, but not every note needs a second brain behind it. These tools all solve the same basic problems in a slightly different way, and they keep the note-taking process simple.

Lightweight, but not barebones

I’m starting off with the least minimalistic option on this list, though it is still quite light and easy to use. QOwnNotes is an open-source cross-platform note-taking app that stores everything in plain text Markdown files on your device. It’s built in C++, making it super lightweight compared to most modern note-takers, and it was originally built around ownCloud integration, though that’s fully optional.

Feature-wise, it covers more ground than most on this list. You get Markdown editing with live preview, tagging, subfolders, full-text search, AES 256 encryption, distraction-free mode, and a browser extension for web clipping. There’s also a scripting engine if you want to customize behavior, and the optional Nextcloud integration for syncing and note versioning across devices. It even has Vim mode if that’s your thing.

Overall, it’s almost as feature-rich as Obsidian but not quite, so there’s less to navigate and you can just focus on creating and managing your notes.

QOwnNotes
See at QOwnNotes

FocusWriter

A truly minimalist note-taking experience

I’ve covered FocusWriter several times already because I just adore it for how distraction-free and easy it is to use. Plus, the aesthetic vibe doesn’t hurt. It’s an open-source word processor developed and maintained by one person, named Graeme Gott. It’s available on Mac, Windows, and Linux and runs locally. The biggest appeal is that the entire interface disappears until you run your cursor to the edge of the screen - it’s just a blank page until then.

The feature set is intentionally minimal. You get autosave, spellcheck, find and replace, daily word and time goals with streak tracking, timers, and real-time stats. By default, it saves your files in ODT format, but it also supports plain text. There’s also a major focus on the look and feel of the app, and you can customize the theme, fonts, colors, and so on.

You’ll probably notice that the functionality is more geared to getting you to focus and write rather than build a knowledge base. So it’s worth keeping that in mind if you’re working with heavy research stacks, in which case, FocusWriter is not the best candidate. But it is an excellent drafting space that makes it easy to focus on whatever it is you’re writing or noting down.

FocusWriter
See at Official Site

Simplenote

It stays true to its name

Despite being almost two decades old, I only discovered Simplenote several months ago, but I’ve been raving about it since. It’s open-source and available on everything, including Windows, Linux, Mac, iOS, Android, and the web. This is a cloud-synced app rather than a local-first one, so you need an account to use it, but it syncs fast and also works offline.

The features are genuinely barebones, which is the whole point. You get a two-pane layout - note list left, editor right - with no toolbar, folders, or formatting buttons. It’s just a blank space to write in. There is Markdown support if you want it but it doesn’t have a live preview that renders the syntax. Tags work for organization if you need them, and version history is pretty solid.

Overall, a very different app from Obsidian. If you’re someone who just needs a simple space for your notes and don’t care about extras like backlinking or dashboard plugins, then Obsidian probably isn’t for you, and something like Simplenote is a much better fit.

Simplenote
See at Simplenote

Joplin

The closest Obsidian alternative

You’ve probably already heard of Joplin, but if not, it’s basically the closest alternative you’ll get to Obsidian. It prioritizes ownership and open standards the same way with its local-first and open standard nature. The look and feel is a little different from Obsidian; its layout is much simpler to navigate and also looks a bit neater.

All your basics are covered. You get tags, notebooks, full-text search, a web clipper, to-do lists with reminders, end-to-end encryption, sync options, and a ton of plugins if you want them (including ones for backlinking and graph views like in Obsidian). It also supports a split Markdown view and live preview mode.

Joplin
See at Joplin

Notes shouldn’t be complicated

Obsidian is worth learning if you want to build a proper knowledge hub, but not every note needs a second brain behind it. These tools all solve the same basic problems in a slightly different way, and they keep the note-taking process simple.

item.title: Microsoft releases new Secure Boot certificate to strengthen system security
item.itunes:title: Microsoft releases new Secure Boot certificate to strengthen system security
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Summary

  • Microsoft will show Secure Boot certificate status in Windows Security starting April 2026 (notification icons: green/yellow/red).
  • Most 2024 PCs don't need the new Secure Boot certification, but older machines will need to update it manually via Windows Update.
  • Windows Security shows green/yellow/red: green means you're good, yellow means manual update, and red means you're blocked by a vulnerability.

In February, Microsoft announced plans to release new Secure Boot certificates through Windows Update. Fast-forward to now, and the tech giant has offered more information on how this works and rolled out the feature.

"Today, Microsoft announced that starting in April 2026, the Windows Security app will display additional information about the status of Secure Boot certificate updates on a device," writes Microsoft in an email to XDA. "This can be found under Device security > Secure Boot to help users easily understand their device’s Secure Boot certificate update status."

With Windows, Secure Boot functionality requires security certificates, and in some cases, these certificates date back to 2011 and need to be updated. If you own a PC from 2024 and earlier, you likely won't need to download a new certificate. However, if your PC is older, you'll need to download this new Secure Boot certificate.

Secure Boot certificate integration with Windows Update is available now

If you have a green check mark in your system tray, you have nothing to worry about

On its support page, Microsoft notes that the Windows Security App and its tray icon will display a green check mark, a yellow band, or a red stop icon to indicate your current certificate status. If it's green, you're good to go, but if it's yellow, you'll need to update the certificate manually. If it's red, you won't be able to obtain a new security certificate due to a vulnerability.

Microsoft says this update is now available, but "additional improvements" are rolling out in May 2026. More details are available on the company's Secure Boot certificate update status page.

Plex gets a fresh look on Fire TV — and a new paywall

You'll now need to pay for a Plex Pass or Remote Watch Pass to stream remotely.

2
By  Patrick O'Rourke

item.title: 3 HDMI settings I always change to unlock my TV’s full potential
item.itunes:title: 3 HDMI settings I always change to unlock my TV’s full potential
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Having recently picked up a 55-inch 4K mini-LED panel with a 144Hz refresh rate, I absolutely love it. It's bright, responsive, and honestly a massive leap over anything I've owned before. But as I started using it more, switching between my console, PC, and streaming apps, I realized that I didn't always feel like I was using all that horsepower.

That's when I dove into the HDMI settings of the TV, and found plenty that were lacking. Of course, there are always a bunch of settings to change with a new TV, but there are also those that are buried a couple of layers deep that a lot of folks never end up touching. Turns out, these toggles make a far bigger difference than any Vivid or Cinema modes ever could.

You don’t need a high-end TV anymore — mid-range TVs are more than good enough

Mid-range TVs offer a lot of high-end features for a fraction of the cost.

4
By  Marshall Gunnell

Enabling Enhanced HDMI or Deep Color

Unlock your TV's actual capabilities

This is the first thing I changed, and the one that most people rarely realize exists in the first place, is very important. Out of the box, most TVs have HDMI ports in a standard mode that prioritizes compatibility over performance. It's designed to smooth over HDMI handshakes, especially with older devices, so you don't end up staring at a blank screen, wondering what went wrong, and demanding a refund at the storefront.

The problem here is that this default mode also ends up capping bandwidth. That means limited color depth, reduced HDR performance, and in some cases, no access to higher refresh rates like 120Hz or 144Hz. You're essentially running your expensive panel in a safe mode that hides what it can really do. This is a setting you need to enable per HDMI port. Your console might be plugged into HDMI 1, your PC into HDMI 2, and each one needs to be manually set to Enhanced. This can also be called a "Deep Color HDMI" setting depending on your TV brand. It's a minor tweak, but it's the difference between having a "nice enough" TV and a properly gorgeous display that you use to the best of its potential.

Enabling HDMI eARC for my soundbar

Audio deserves the same bandwidth as the visuals

If you're using a soundbar or an AV receiver, chances are that your audio is actually being compressed without you realizing it. Standard ARC (Audio Return Channel) simply doesn't have the bandwidth to carry high-bitrate, uncompressed audio formats. That's where eARC (enhanced ARC) comes in. It allows your TV to send full-quality audio back through the same HDMI cable to your soundbar, and it even includes formats like Dolby Atmos and DTS:X.

With eARC, you get no compromises or downscaling in your audio quality, and it's a rather experiential difference. Dialogue feels immediately clearer, spatial audio becomes way more precise, and big, cinematic moments (I watch Godzilla doing something cool once every week) actually hit home in a way that standard ARC just doesn't deliver. Again, eARC isn't enabled by default, so if you've invested in good audio gear, this is the toggle that ensures you'll actually hear what you've paid all that money for.

Your soundbar won't talk to your TV, and manufacturers act like that's normal

HDMI eARC was supposed to simplify home theater, but unreliable handshakes, sync issues, and audio dropouts still make it feel unfinished.

5
By  Jeff Butts

Turning on ALLM for gaming

Your TV shouldn't be slowing your inputs down

If you game on your TV, even casually (or religiously like I do), ALLM is non-negotiable. ALLM, or Auto Low Latency Mode, ensures that your TV switches into its lowest-latency profile the moment it detects a gaming signal. Instead of digging through menus, or picking up the remote to toggle Game Mode on and off, ALLM makes sure that everything works just as well as it should.

Without it, your TV might still be applying image processing in the background. Things like motion interpolation, noise reduction, and sharpening don't sound harmful, but they do end up adding an amount of input lag. Once you notice that slight delay between pressing a button and seeing it on screen, it becomes rather impossible not to see and ignore. The beauty of ALLM is that it removes that friction completely. Your console tells the TV it's supposed to be displaying a game, and the TV ensures that everything feels tighter, more immediate, and far more in sync with what your hands (and controller) are doing.

I changed a few Google TV settings, and gaming finally felt responsive

Your TV might be adding extra input lag, and fixing it is easy

By  Abid Ahsan Shanto

Use the right HDMI port for the right device

Not all HDMI ports on your TV are created equal

This one isn't really a setting, but it is very well the most overlooked mistake I see people make. Not all HDMI ports on your TV are identical. While some likely support full HDMI 2.1 bandwidth, others are limited to HDMI 2.0, and one is usually reserved for eARC. With this in mind, high-end gaming PCs or consoles are supposed to go into the port that supports 4K at high refresh rates. Plug it into the wrong one, and you might be capped at 4K60Hz instead of 120Hz. Heck, you might not even see options like VRR show up.

Subscribe for hidden TV and HDMI setup tips

Join the newsletter to get clear, hands-on guides that uncover hidden TV toggles, HDMI port pairing, and audio setup tricks — plus practical device optimization tips and other useful tech how-tos.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

It sounds obvious, but in practice, it's easy to overlook, especially when all the ports look the same. Taking a minute to match each device to the right port ensures everything else you’ve configured actually works as intended. Otherwise, you're solving the right problem in the wrong place.

TCL 65-inch Class 6-Series 4K Mini-LED UHD Smart Roku TV
See at Best Buy
Forget OLED — mini-LED is the real future of displays

OLED is great, but mini-LED is still better in several ways.

13
By  Jasmine Mannan

You might not be fully using what you've paid for

It's rather surprising how uncomplicated these settings are, but that they're still hidden for a majority of users. They're not flashy, nor are they marketed front and center, and yet, they can fundamentally change your TV's entire viewing experience altogether.

After making these changes, you can feel everything click into place. The responsiveness, the clarity, and the audio will all start to feel more cohesive. That's really what you're chasing when you invest in good hardware: a setup that convinces you that you're getting the most for your money.

I changed a few Google TV settings, and gaming finally felt responsive

By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

It sounds obvious, but in practice, it's easy to overlook, especially when all the ports look the same. Taking a minute to match each device to the right port ensures everything else you’ve configured actually works as intended. Otherwise, you're solving the right problem in the wrong place.

TCL 65-inch Class 6-Series 4K Mini-LED UHD Smart Roku TV
See at Best Buy
Forget OLED — mini-LED is the real future of displays

OLED is great, but mini-LED is still better in several ways.

13

item.title: I thought a Wi-Fi Extender would help my network — it did, just not the way I expected
item.itunes:title: I thought a Wi-Fi Extender would help my network — it did, just not the way I expected
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Before my ISP finally replaced my Wi-Fi 5 router, I struggled with a dead zone inside the apartment where the Wi-Fi dropped to unusable speeds, often disconnecting entirely. This spot was in the bedroom furthest from the router, proving too much for its limited capabilities. After ignoring the problem for months (since I spent only an hour or so in the bedroom before sleeping), I had had enough. I decided that a cheap extender would be the easiest solution. I knew it was more of a band-aid than a proper fix, but if it solved this one issue, my home Wi-Fi would have been near-perfect. As it happens, the Wi-Fi extender technically improved the Wi-Fi speed in the bedroom, but the real difference was negligible. It was as if the extender did a whole lot of nothing, and that's when I understood why people prefer wired access points and mesh networks so much.

4 reasons you should use a mesh network instead of a Wi-Fi extender

Don't get stuck in the past, consider upgrading to a mesh network instead of using the band-aid of a Wi-Fi extender in your home.

By  Joe Rice-Jones

My Wi-Fi 5 router just couldn't cover the entire house

I guess Wi-Fi 5 wasn't sufficient after all

Wi-Fi 5 routers should not really be prevalent in 2026, but my ISP refuses to upgrade old customers to one of the Wi-Fi 6 routers they readily provide to new subscribers. Hence, I was forced to use a Wi-Fi 5 router for almost six years, during which I faced more than a few connectivity problems. The biggest annoyance was the dead spot I mentioned before. The router was probably too underpowered to cover the 1600 sq. ft. apartment, which isn't a lot, by the way. If the door to the bedroom was closed, the furthest spot in the room only got around 4.5 Mbps of speed. Even on the 2.4GHz band, the latency was so bad that my phone sometimes struggled to send WhatsApp messages. Other times, the phone switched entirely to mobile data, giving up on the connection entirely.

Then, there was the bizarre issue of my PC's Ethernet link speed occasionally dropping to 100 Mbps, essentially halving my internet speed. Most of the time, it enjoyed the full 200 Mbps speed of the internet plan, but this link speed downgrade started happening more often than I could stomach. Even the WPA2 security standard of the router was far from ideal, leaving my connection exposed to external exploits. This was the time I had given up on my ISP replacing the router, so I went after the most burning problem of the network — the dead spot. I didn't want to invest in a mesh network yet, as even a Wi-Fi 5 router is supposed to cover 1600 sq. ft. easily. Hence, I decided to start with a cheap and simple solution: a Wi-Fi extender.

5 signs your Wi-Fi router is officially too old

Unsure if your router is due an upgrade? Look out for these signs that it might be time to make a change

5
By  Elliot Alexander

Wi-Fi extenders aren't perfect, but they couldn't be that bad

Or so I thought

Between buying an aftermarket router, investing in a mesh network, and setting up a wired access point, nothing seemed particularly appealing to me. I didn't want to commit to a costly fix, since I hadn't yet tried the cheapest one. I knew a Wi-Fi extender wouldn't magically get rid of all the connectivity issues, but I hoped it would at least improve the range of the network. So, I bought a $20 extender from TP-Link, set it up with the home network, and waited to see the magic happen. When I measured the Wi-Fi speed with the same test in the same spot, I saw an eight-fold increase, but the reality was very different.

The connection didn't feel any more responsive or faster than before. The extender was receiving signals from the router and rebroadcasting them to the furthest room, but this wasn't creating the desired outcome. Perhaps the only silver lining here was that the extender was functioning just like it was designed to. Since Wi-Fi extenders need to connect to the router and then bounce that signal to your devices, this doubles the traffic in both handshakes — device to extender and extender to router. Combined with the fact that an extender needs proximity to the router for adequate signal strength, this means that extenders are designed to fail. They can be helpful in some situations where the connectivity gap you're trying to bridge is short enough, but not when you expect a high-speed Wi-Fi extension to far-away dead zones.

I finally upgraded to a mesh Wi-Fi system and it made me realize Wi-Fi extenders are a scam

If I'm paying for high speeds, I should be able to get them all over my home.

6
By  Jasmine Mannan

My Wi-Fi 6 router is an improvement, but I may need other upgrades

It's only a matter of time

I kept trying to get my ISP to replace my Wi-Fi 5 router, and lo and behold, they finally agreed last month. I got a Wi-Fi 6 router after six years of using an underpowered Wi-Fi 5 model, and I had great expectations. The expanded range of the new router instantly fixed the dead spot in the bedroom, so the upgrade was definitely working. That said, the new router didn't really make the network that much more responsive or efficient. Perhaps it's the low number of devices in the house, the lack of a Gigabit plan, or the fact that Wi-Fi 5 was already good enough except for that one dead spot. Still, my home network is now equipped with a modern router and ready for future speed upgrades and high-bandwidth devices like a NAS or home server.

Subscribe to our newsletter for clear Wi‑Fi upgrade guidance

Get deeper hands-on Wi‑Fi expertise by subscribing to our newsletter: practical troubleshooting steps, clear comparisons of extenders, mesh, and wired access points, and straightforward upgrade guidance to help you make informed home-network decisions.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

My Wi-Fi range is much better now, but I might still need to get a mesh network or wired access points in the future. A wired connection for my smart TV will make streaming significantly better, getting rid of the occasional weirdness that still happens on Wi-Fi. A mesh node will boost the connection speed in the bedroom, especially one with a wired backhaul. These upgrades will cost more in the short term, but provide long-lasting benefits. They may not prove to be as radical as one might expect, but they'll certainly make my home network more evolved, flexible, and reliable.

TP-Link Deco X68

TP-Link's new AX3600 (Deco X68) Tri-Band WiFi 6 mesh router lives up to its range promise, but I wasn't able to see the advertised speeds and want TP-Link to address the unexplained data transmissions and software issues with the Deco app

$200 at Amazon
I ran wired backhaul to every room in my house, and it was so worth it

After years of being Wi-Fi first, now I've seen the light

17
By  Joe Rice-Jones

Wi-Fi extenders are rarely a good option

While a Wi-Fi extender can be a cheap solution to increase the range of your network, it seldom works as you'd like it to. It can technically increase the network speed to the dead zones inside your house, but the way it communicates with your router makes the connection sluggish. You will probably feel no major difference after installing an extender. Better solutions are wired access points or mesh nodes that use advanced methods to offer true upgrades to your router's capabilities.
item.description:
Before my ISP finally replaced my Wi-Fi 5 router, I struggled with a dead zone inside the apartment where the Wi-Fi dropped to unusable speeds, often disconnecting entirely. This spot was in the bedroom furthest from the router, proving too much for its limited capabilities. After ignoring the problem for months (since I spent only an hour or so in the bedroom before sleeping), I had had enough. I decided that a cheap extender would be the easiest solution. I knew it was more of a band-aid than a proper fix, but if it solved this one issue, my home Wi-Fi would have been near-perfect. As it happens, the Wi-Fi extender technically improved the Wi-Fi speed in the bedroom, but the real difference was negligible. It was as if the extender did a whole lot of nothing, and that's when I understood why people prefer wired access points and mesh networks so much.

4 reasons you should use a mesh network instead of a Wi-Fi extender

Don't get stuck in the past, consider upgrading to a mesh network instead of using the band-aid of a Wi-Fi extender in your home.

By  Joe Rice-Jones

My Wi-Fi 5 router just couldn't cover the entire house

I guess Wi-Fi 5 wasn't sufficient after all

Wi-Fi 5 routers should not really be prevalent in 2026, but my ISP refuses to upgrade old customers to one of the Wi-Fi 6 routers they readily provide to new subscribers. Hence, I was forced to use a Wi-Fi 5 router for almost six years, during which I faced more than a few connectivity problems. The biggest annoyance was the dead spot I mentioned before. The router was probably too underpowered to cover the 1600 sq. ft. apartment, which isn't a lot, by the way. If the door to the bedroom was closed, the furthest spot in the room only got around 4.5 Mbps of speed. Even on the 2.4GHz band, the latency was so bad that my phone sometimes struggled to send WhatsApp messages. Other times, the phone switched entirely to mobile data, giving up on the connection entirely.

Most of the time, it enjoyed the full 200 Mbps speed of the internet plan, but this link speed downgrade started happening more often than I could stomach. Even the WPA2 security standard of the router was far from ideal, leaving my connection exposed to external exploits. This was the time I had given up on my ISP replacing the router, so I went after the most burning problem of the network — the dead spot. I didn't want to invest in a mesh network yet, as even a Wi-Fi 5 router is supposed to cover 1600 sq. ft. easily. Hence, I decided to start with a cheap and simple solution: a Wi-Fi extender.
